That was naive of me. Now I see it everywhere—doctored photos, stories that don't add up, people claiming things that just don't make sense. It worries me, honestly. My grandson showed me how easy it is to fake things with computers now, and that made me anxious about what I'm seeing.

What I've learned is you have to slow down and think before you believe something, especially if it upsets you or makes you angry right away. When I see something on Facebook that troubles me, I try to take a breath—I do my yoga breathing, actually—and ask myself if I've heard this from a trusted source before. I check if the news comes from places I recognize, like the actual news stations I watched for fifty years. If it's just some random person sharing something wild, I'm skeptical now.

I've started asking my son or my volunteer coordinator at the community center about things I'm unsure of. They're younger and more tech-savvy than me. There's no shame in asking. Sometimes I'll see a photo that looks too perfect or a story about a celebrity that seems made up, and I just don't share it. I used to think I was helping people by spreading information, but now I realize I might be spreading lies instead.

The thing that helps me most is remembering that real life, the one I live here in New York with my knitting and my volunteering, is what matters. Social media is just a small part of my week anyway. response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend roughly a few times weekly on social media

response_parsed.opinion:
You know, when I first got on social media a few years back, I thought everyone was telling the truth like they would face-to-face. That was naive of me. Now I see it everywhere—doctored photos, stories that don't add up, people claiming things that just don't make sense. It worries me, honestly. My grandson showed me how easy it is to fake things with computers now, and that made me anxious about what I'm seeing.

What I've learned is you have to slow down and think before you believe something, especially if it upsets you or makes you angry right away. When I see something on Facebook that troubles me, I try to take a breath—I do my yoga breathing, actually—and ask myself if I've heard this from a trusted source before. I check if the news comes from places I recognize, like the actual news stations I watched for fifty years. If it's just some random person sharing something wild, I'm skeptical now.

I've started asking my son or my volunteer coordinator at the community center about things I'm unsure of. They're younger and more tech-savvy than me. There's no shame in asking. Sometimes I'll see a photo that looks too perfect or a story about a celebrity that seems made up, and I just don't share it. I used to think I was helping people by spreading information, but now I realize I might be spreading lies instead.

The thing that helps me most is remembering that real life, the one I live here in New York with my knitting and my volunteering, is what matters. Social media is just a small part of my week anyway. I don't spend hours on it. When something feels off or makes me deeply uneasy, I trust that feeling. God gave us common sense for a reason. I'm careful now, more careful than before, and I sleep better knowing I'm not passing along falsehoods to people I care about.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is an 80-year-old retired man with lower secondary education, high neuroticism (86), low conscientiousness (15), and moderate openness (43). His anxiety about fake content stems from his high neuroticism and low education level, making him vulnerable but also cautious. His creative nature and spiritual beliefs inform his approach. His low extraversion and moderate agreeableness suggest he's reflective rather than confrontational. His infrequent social media use and hobbies like yoga indicate he can step back from digital stress. The response emphasizes personal verification, trusted relationships, emotional awareness, and the grounding effect of real-world activities—all aligned with his profile.
response_parsed.tone: Reflective, slightly anxious but increasingly confident, humble and willing to learn, warm and personal, grounded in real-world values and spiritual perspective, conversational and genuine without pretension
